aliases: 参峇魔鬼鱼 (Shēn Bā Mógui Yú)

chinese: 参峇魔鬼鱼 (Shēn Bā Mógui Yú)

food_subgroup: Grilled stingray fillet with sambal chilli paste, served on banana leaf. Reference serving: 200g fillet including sambal coating.

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for sambal stingray as a composite dish. Closest parent concept: grilled fish with chilli paste (FOODON:03302051 — fish food product). No exact match available as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rays and skates (Rajiformes) — closest FoodEx2 anchor for stingray species; sambal preparation not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers rays and skates as a species group. The sambal-grilled preparation and composite dish are not separately coded in FoodEx2 as of April 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for sambal stingray dish. SNOMED CT does not cover composite hawker preparations at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker portion of sambal stingray is approximately 200g, comprising a stingray wing fillet grilled on banana leaf and topped with a generous layer of sambal chilli paste.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal Stingray — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo / My Healthy Plate)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for sambal stingray. Values represent the composite dish (stingray fillet + sambal coating) per 100g. HPB Foodo is an approved primary source for Singapore hawker dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Fish and Seafood Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal stingray in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on the dish's macronutrient profile: predominantly lean protein (15g/100g) and low carbohydrate (3g/100g) from sambal paste ingredients (chilli, shallots, garlic). The carbohydrate fraction is predominantly from non-starchy vegetables and spice paste, consistent with low-GI analogues in the University of Sydney database. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (30 × 6g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.8 (very low). This estimation is consistent with HPB Singapore dietary guidance for grilled seafood dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 320mg per 100g (640mg per 200g serving), primarily from belacan (fermented shrimp paste) in the sambal
hypertension.guidance: Sambal stingray provides approximately 640mg sodium per standard 200g serving, with the majority contributed by belacan and chilli paste; individuals with hypertension should request reduced sambal and avoid additional dipping sauces such as chincalok to keep total sodium within recommended limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic load (GL ~1.8 per 200g serving) and high lean protein content (30g per 200g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal stingray is an excellent hawker choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic load of approximately 1.8 per serving, high lean protein content of 30g per 200g portion, and grilled preparation with no added oil, all of which support postprandial blood glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.8g/100g) and moderate sodium (320mg/100g); grilled preparation avoids added cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sambal stingray is a relatively heart-friendly hawker option given its low saturated fat content of 0.8g per 100g and grilled preparation without added oil; however, the moderate sodium load of 640mg per 200g serving warrants portion awareness for individuals managing cardiovascular risk, and the selenium content (30mcg/100g) provides antioxidant support.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate omega-3 content, good selenium and vitamin B12, low saturated fat; capsaicin in sambal has mild analgesic properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Stingray provides moderate omega-3 fatty acids (~0.3g EPA+DHA per 200g serving), good selenium (30mcg/100g) and vitamin B12 (2.0mcg/100g) supporting anti-inflammatory status, and the capsaicin in sambal chilli may offer mild analgesic benefit for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Stingray (Ikan Pari Bakar) is a quintessential Singapore and Malaysian hawker dish consisting of a firm, boneless stingray wing fillet grilled over charcoal on a banana leaf and topped with a generous layer of fragrant sambal chilli paste. The banana leaf acts as a natural cooking surface, imparting a subtle smoky-grassy aroma while preventing the delicate fish from sticking to the grill. The sambal is a complex spice paste typically made from dried chilli peppers, belacan (fermented shrimp paste), shallots, garlic, and sometimes lemongrass or ginger, providing the dish's characteristic bold, spicy, and umami-rich flavour. Stingray flesh is firm, white, mildly flavoured, and entirely boneless, making it easy to eat. The dish is almost always served with calamansi lime and sometimes accompanied by chincalok (fermented small shrimp sauce) as a tangy dip. Nutritionally, sambal stingray is an excellent lean protein source at 15g per 100g (30g per 200g serving), low in fat (3g/100g), and low in saturated fat (0.8g/100g). The grilled preparation uses no added cooking oil. Sodium is moderate at 320mg per 100g, primarily from belacan in the sambal. The dish is a good source of selenium (30mcg/100g), vitamin B12 (2.0mcg/100g), niacin (5mg/100g), and phosphorus (200mg/100g). Glycemic load is very low (~1.8 per 200g serving), making it one of the better hawker choices for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Halal-certified, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Available at virtually every Singapore seafood hawker stall and commonly ordered as a shared dish in group meals.
common_user_queries: is sambal stingray good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the approved primary source for Singapore hawker dishes under Project Veritas v2. The dish is a well-documented canonical Singapore hawker item with consistent nutritional characterisation across HPB records. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology, documented as such.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for sambal stingray as a composite dish is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite sambal stingray dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of April 2026. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source — grilled fish preparation with no hydrogenated fats). No core macro is zero: protein_g 15.0, fat_g 3.0, carbohydrate_g 3.0 are all non-zero and sourced from HPB Singapore. GI value of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for sambal stingray exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Portions: the canonical reference serving is 200g (stingray fillet + sambal coating); a 300g serving variant is noted for larger portions. Duplicate 300g portion entries in input have been deduplicated. Tamil and Hindi local names are transliterations/adaptations as no standardised culinary Tamil or Hindi term for this dish exists in published food composition databases; forms provided represent the most natural phonetic rendering for retrieval purposes. Chinese traditional form 參巴魔鬼魚 is a confident conversion of the simplified form 参巴魔鬼鱼.
